title: Does Systema-Style Perturbed-Reference Residualization Help as a Training Target for Unseen Single-Cell Perturbation Prediction? A Pre-Registered Benchmark Note
description: This project is a benchmark note, not a new method paper. It tests one narrow question: whether training on a train-only Systema-style perturbed-reference residual improves unseen single-cell perturbation prediction relative to full-target training and strong simple baselines, with retrieval treated only as a secondary add-on.
motivation: Recent perturbation benchmarks show that simple baselines such as train mean and median remain competitive, while Systema argues that standard evaluation can over-credit shared systematic variation. The missing benchmark question is whether the same perturbed-reference idea is useful as a training target, under fixed lightweight features and a realistic small-compute budget.
proposed_approach: Pre-register the primary residual target as r_sys = x_pert - mu_pert_train, where mu_pert_train is the train-only mean perturbation centroid after splitting. Evaluate a fixed nine-model ladder on Adamson and Replogle K562 Essential across seeds {11, 23, 37}: Train Perturbed Mean, Train Perturbed Median, Non-residualized Ridge, Residualized Ridge, Residualized PLS, Residualized Linear Embedding, Retrieval-only Residual kNN, ReSRP-Linear, and ReSRP-MLP. Use seed-averaged differences in perturbed-reference Pearson as the primary endpoint, RMSE as a co-primary guardrail, and keep composite rank only as secondary descriptive support. Add a pre-registered feature-sufficiency contingency by comparing true STRING descriptors against permuted and degree-only descriptors so null results are not misattributed to residualization if perturbation features are too weak.

related_work.title: Systema: a framework for evaluating genetic perturbation response prediction beyond systematic variation
related_work.authors: Ramon Vinas Torne, Michal Wiatrak, Zoe Piran, Shuyang Fan, Liangze Jiang, Sarah A. Teichmann, Mor Nitzan, Maria Brbic
related_work.how_it_relates: Systema is the direct conceptual precursor. It motivates perturbed-reference evaluation, and this proposal asks the narrower benchmark question of whether that same reference should also define the training target.

related_work.title: A comparison of computational methods for expression forecasting
related_work.authors: Eric Kernfeld, Yunxiao Yang, Joshua S. Weinstock, Alexis Battle, Patrick Cahan
related_work.how_it_relates: This 2025 benchmark is the main motivation for the paper's benchmark-note framing because it shows that simple baselines can remain surprisingly competitive and that benchmark design choices strongly affect conclusions.

related_work.title: Benchmarking algorithms for generalizable single-cell perturbation response prediction
related_work.how_it_relates: This paper provides processed benchmark assets and standardized unseen-perturbation settings that make a controlled, reproducible lightweight study feasible.

related_work.title: Benchmarking foundation cell models for post-perturbation RNA-seq prediction
related_work.authors: Gabor Csendes, Gergo Sanz, Katalin Z. Szalay, Balazs Szalai
related_work.how_it_relates: This benchmark reinforces that larger pretrained models do not reliably dominate trivial baselines, supporting a paper whose main contribution is sharper experimental design rather than scale.

related_work.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Predicting Cellular Responses to Gene Perturbation
related_work.authors: Andrea Giuseppe Di Francesco, Andrea Rubbi, Pietro Lio
related_work.how_it_relates: PT-RAG shows that retrieval-augmented perturbation prediction is already an active line of work. That lowers novelty around retrieval itself and justifies treating retrieval only as a secondary add-on in this proposal.

related_work.title: TxPert: Leveraging Biochemical Relationships for Out-of-Distribution Transcriptomic Perturbation Prediction
related_work.how_it_relates: TxPert motivates the use of cheap biological relational priors. This proposal adopts that lesson in a bounded way through fixed STRING descriptors rather than proposing a new graph architecture.

related_work.title: Causal Cellular Context Transfer Learning (C3TL): An Efficient Architecture for Prediction of Unseen Perturbation Effects
related_work.how_it_relates: C3TL is relevant as an efficient unseen-perturbation predictor, but this proposal differs by studying target definition rather than a new transfer mechanism.

hypothesis: Training on a train-only perturbed-reference residual target will improve unseen perturbation prediction over full-target training on directly interpretable metrics, particularly perturbed-reference Pearson, without worsening reconstructed-centroid RMSE. Retrieval may add further gain, but only if it beats the strongest residualized non-retrieval baseline under the same target definition.
success_criteria: Residualized Ridge improves perturbed-reference Pearson over Non-residualized Ridge on both Adamson and Replogle K562 Essential.
The same residualization comparison does not materially worsen RMSE; the seed-averaged RMSE difference is non-positive or negligible on both datasets.

At least one residualized non-retrieval baseline from {Residualized Ridge, Residualized PLS, Residualized Linear Embedding} also beats Train Perturbed Mean on perturbed-reference Pearson on both datasets.
The best retrieval model from {ReSRP-Linear, ReSRP-MLP} beats the selected best residualized non-retrieval baseline on perturbed-reference Pearson on both datasets without worsening RMSE.
Retrieval-only Residual kNN does not match the winning ReSRP variant, so any retrieval gain cannot be explained by neighbor averaging alone.

If true STRING descriptors fail to outperform permuted or degree-only descriptors, any null residualization result is reported as inconclusive due to weak perturbation features rather than as evidence against residualization.
